title: Building a field shelter from treated timber
subtitle: A three-sided open-fronted shelter for horses, cattle, or large stock.
excerpt: A three-sided field shelter protects stock from wind and rain without the ventilation problems of a fully enclosed building. This is a construction guide for a standard 3.6 m by 2.4 m treated-timber frame shelter suitable for two to four animals, covering post setting, frame construction, and roof sheeting.
sourceNotes: RSPCA equine shelter guidelines. Farmers Weekly, field shelter construction guidance. Smallholder Magazine, shelter project guides. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), farm building chapters.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber impregnated with preservative under pressure so the treatment penetrates the wood cells. Provides resistance to rot and insect damage. Required for any outdoor structural timber in contact with the ground or regular moisture.
A construction method where vertical posts are driven or set in the ground and horizontal rails are fixed between them. The primary structural system for field shelters and paddock fencing.

content.text: A 3.6 m by 2.4 m shelter is a manageable first build: four upright posts, a ridge beam, four rafters, and a corrugated sheet roof. All timber should be
for ground contact. The open front should face away from the prevailing wind, typically away from the south-west in the UK.

Four 100 mm by 100 mm uprights at 3 m length (1 m in the ground, 2 m above). Three 100 mm by 50 mm horizontal rails per wall for each side and the back. One ridge beam at 100 mm by 100 mm, 3.6 m long. Four rafters at 100 mm by 50 mm, pitched for a minimum 10-degree roof slope. Corrugated metal or polycarbonate roofing sheets to cover the 3.6 m by 2.4 m roof area plus 300 mm overhang front and back. Galvanised post anchors or concrete for the posts.
Mark the four post positions with a string line and level. Using a

construction, ram or dig posts to 1 m depth; posts set shallower will lean under wind load with a roof attached. Set the two rear posts 200 mm lower than the front posts to create the roof pitch. On softer ground, concrete the four corner posts. Check posts for vertical and diagonal level before the concrete sets.

Fix the ridge beam across the top of the two front posts, then the horizontal rail across the rear posts. Add horizontal rails to the two side walls at 300 to 400 mm clear spacing between rails to prevent stock forcing through. Fix the rafters between ridge and rear rail at equal spacing. Fix roofing sheets with appropriate roofing screws through the corrugation crests, not the troughs. Overlap sheets by one corrugation at the sides and 150 mm at the ridge line.

attrs.title: Planning permission
attrs.body: Moveable temporary field shelters on skids rather than foundations generally do not require planning permission. A permanently founded shelter may need permitted development assessment. Check with your local planning authority before building.
